name: Neuro On-Call Quick References
html:
💊

Seizure Medications & Dosing

Benzodiazepine dosing and weight-based reference guides

Drug Initial dose Target dose Blood conc. (µg/mL) Half‑life (h) Major contraindications / cautions
Brivaracetam1–2 mg/kg/day2–4 mg/kg/day2–107–8Prior serious hypersensitivity to levetiracetam/brivaracetam; significant hepatic impairment (adjust dose)
Cannabidiol (Epidiolex)5 mg/kg/day10–20 mg/kg/day divided BID†50–60Significant hepatic impairment without monitoring; interactions (esp. valproate, clobazam); monitor LFTs
Cenobamate (Xcopri)~0.5–1 mg/kg/day (slow titration)3–5 mg/kg/day (max ~200 mg/day in adolescents)†50–60Familial short‑QT syndrome, DRESS with rapid titration, significant hepatic impairment
Clobazam0.1–0.2 mg/kg/day0.3–1 mg/kg/day†18–50Severe hepatic impairment, significant respiratory depression, other sedatives
Clonazepam0.02 mg/kg/day0.5–1 mg/day*20–40Significant respiratory depression, severe liver disease
Ethosuximide10 mg/kg/day15–40 mg/kg/day50–10030–40Porphyria, severe hepatic or renal impairment
Felbamate15 mg/kg/day15–45 mg/kg/day40–8020–23History of aplastic anemia or hepatic failure
Fenfluramine0.1 mg/kg/day0.35–0.7 mg/kg/day (per label max)†20–30Valvular heart disease or pulmonary arterial hypertension; requires echo monitoring
Fosphenytoin (IV, as PE)5–10 mg/kg/day equivalent5–10 mg/kg/day10–2524Sinus bradycardia, SA block, 2nd/3rd‑degree AV block, prior phenytoin hypersensitivity
Gabapentin10–15 mg/kg/day30–50 mg/kg/day divided TID†5–7Severe renal impairment without dose adjustment; caution with CNS depressants
Lacosamide2–3 mg/kg/day6–12 mg/kg/day10–2012–162nd/3rd‑degree AV block, significant PR prolongation or conduction disease
Lamotrigine (non‑valproate)0.5 mg/kg/day5–10 mg/kg/day2–2025 (monotherapy)Prior serious rash (SJS/TEN), rapid titration, valproate co‑use without dose adjustment
Levetiracetam30 mg/kg/day60 mg/kg/day15–405Behavioral toxicity; adjust in renal impairment
Oxcarbazepine10 mg/kg/day20–40 mg/kg/day10–409Prior carbamazepine hypersensitivity, HLA‑B*1502 risk, hyponatremia
Phenobarbital3–5 mg/kg/day5–10 mg/kg/day15–4035–73Severe respiratory depression, porphyria, advanced hepatic impairment
Rufinamide10 mg/kg/day30–45 mg/kg/day divided BID5–306–10Familial short‑QT syndrome; caution in hepatic impairment and with QT‑affecting drugs
Stiripentol25 mg/kg/day50–75 mg/kg/day divided BID–TID†4–13Hepatic/renal impairment; increases clobazam/valproate levels
Topiramate1–3 mg/kg/day5–9 mg/kg/day†18–30Kidney stones, metabolic acidosis, glaucoma; adjust in renal impairment
Valproate20 mg/kg/day30–60 mg/kg/day50–1006–15Pregnancy, urea‑cycle defects, significant liver disease, POLG‑related disorders
Vigabatrin40–60 mg/kg/day60–80 mg/kg/day†DaysPre‑existing serious visual field loss; vision toxicity risk
Zonisamide1.5 mg/kg/day6 mg/kg/day10–4024–60Sulfonamide allergy, kidney stones, metabolic acidosis, oligohidrosis/heat‑stroke risk

* No routine therapeutic range.   † No well‑established serum therapeutic range; monitoring is clinical.

Use age‑based concentration and weight‑based dose (max 20 mg).

Valtoco (Intranasal Diazepam) – Children ≥6 Years

Individualized dosing based on age and weight (max 20 mg per dose).

Ages 6–11 years (0.3 mg/kg; 0.66 mg/lb)

Weight (kg)Dose (mg)Given as
10–185One 5‑mg nasal spray device in one nostril
19–3710One 10‑mg nasal spray device in one nostril
38–5515Two 7.5‑mg nasal spray devices, one in each nostril
56–7420Two 10‑mg nasal spray devices, one in each nostril

Ages ≥12 years (0.2 mg/kg; 0.44 mg/lb)

Weight (kg)Dose (mg)Given as
14–275One 5‑mg nasal spray device in one nostril
28–5010One 10‑mg nasal spray device in one nostril
51–7515Two 7.5‑mg nasal spray devices, one in each nostril
≥7620Two 10‑mg nasal spray devices, one in each nostril

Benzodiazepines for Seizures – Quick Reference

Medication Adult dosing Pediatric dosing Key adverse effects
Diazepam (IV / PR) 0.15 mg/kg IV (max 10 mg per dose); may redose in 5 min PR: 2–5 yrs 0.5 mg/kg; 6–11 yrs 0.3 mg/kg; ≥12 yrs 0.2 mg/kg (see Diastat/Valtoco tables for exact mg) Respiratory depression; hypotension and bradycardia (propylene glycol–containing IV)
Lorazepam (IV) 0.1 mg/kg IV (max 4 mg per dose); may redose in 10 min 0.1 mg/kg IV (max 4 mg per dose); may redose in 10 min Respiratory depression; hypotension and bradycardia (propylene glycol–containing IV)
Midazolam (IV / IM / IN / buccal) 0.2 mg/kg IV or IM (max 10 mg); may redose in 10 min IM: 10 mg if >40 kg; 5 mg if 13–40 kg. IN: 0.2 mg/kg. Buccal: 0.5 mg/kg Respiratory depression; hypotension
⚖️

Benzodiazepine Equivalency Table

Approximate oral dose equivalents and properties

Name (generic / brand) Approx. dose equivalent to 10 mg diazepam Common tablet strengths Duration of action* Half‑life** (hours)
Alprazolam (Xanax)0.5–1 mg0.25 mg, 0.5 mg, 1 mg, 2 mg (XR 0.5–3 mg)Short6–26 (XR ~10–16)
Clobazam (Onfi, Sympazan)20 mg10 mg, 20 mgVery long>100
Clonazepam (Klonopin)0.5 mg0.125 mg, 0.25 mg (ODT), 0.5 mg, 1 mg, 2 mgLong18–50
Diazepam (Valium)10 mg2 mg, 5 mg, 10 mgVery long20–80
Lorazepam (Ativan)1–2 mg0.5 mg, 1 mg, 2 mgIntermediate10–20

* Very short <6 h, short ~6–12 h, intermediate ~12–24 h, long ~24–48 h, very long >48 h.
** Includes active metabolites where relevant.
